patient_information.allergies: Penicillin (develops diffuse pruritic rash and hives within 1–2 hours of exposure, no anaphylaxis to date). Seasonal environmental allergies to grass and tree pollens (nasal congestion, itchy eyes, mild wheezing during high pollen counts). No known food allergies.

patient_insurance_information.primary_medical_insurance: Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield of Virginia

certificate_of_medical_necessity.other_diagnosis: J45.50 – Severe persistent asthma, uncomplicated, characterized by daily symptoms, nocturnal awakenings 3–4 times per week prior to optimized therapy, FEV1 consistently <60% predicted despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroid/long-acting beta-agonist (ICS/LABA) therapy, and two oral corticosteroid-requiring exacerbations in the past 12 months. Co-morbid allergic rhinitis (J30.1) with positive skin prick testing to dust mites, grasses, and tree pollens. No history of COPD, bronchiectasis, or other chronic lung disease. Non-smoker, no vaping history.

prescription_information_table.direction: Inject 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks (two 150 mg prefilled syringes administered consecutively at each visit). First three doses to be administered in the allergy clinic with 2-hour post-injection observation due to risk of anaphylaxis. Subsequent doses may be given in office with at least 30 minutes of observation if prior doses are tolerated without systemic reaction.

prescription_information.direction: Inject 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks (two 150 mg syringes per dose) administered in the allergist’s office under supervision, with post-injection monitoring per clinic protocol.
prescription_information.xolair_others: Dose selection based on pre-treatment serum IgE of 310 IU/mL and body weight of 82 kg, consistent with current dosing guidelines for severe persistent allergic asthma in adults. Administer in a healthcare setting equipped to manage anaphylaxis. Maintain patient on background controller therapy including high-dose ICS/LABA and as-needed rescue albuterol. Reassess asthma control, exacerbation frequency, and lung function (spirometry) after 4–6 months of therapy to determine ongoing need and effectiveness.
